contents: SYSTEMATIC REVIEWBJDBritish Journal of DermatologyRisks for Staphylococcus aureus colonization in patientswith psoriasis: a systematic review and meta-analysisC.Y. Ng,1,2,3 Y.H. Huang,1,2 C.F. Chu,2 T.C. Wu2 and S.H. Liu iD 2,41Department of Dermatology and 4Department of Family Medicine, Chang Gung Memorial Hospital, Linkou, Taoyuan City, Taiwan2College of Medicine and 3Graduate Institute of Clinical Medical Sciences, Chang Gung University, Gueishan District, Taoyuan City, TaiwanLinked Comment: Hay. Br J Dermatol 2017; 177:894-895.CorrespondenceSu-Hsun Liu.E-mail: Accepted for publication22 January 2017Funding sourcesNone.

Conflicts of interestNone declared.SummaryEvidence on whether patients with psoriasis have a higher risk for staphylococcalcolonization than healthy controls remains controversial. To synthesize the currentliterature, we performed a systematic review on the prevalence and relative risk(RR) of Staphylococcus aureus colonization in patients with psoriasis. We modified theQUADAS-2 instrument to assess the reporting quality of individual studies andapplied random-effects models in meta-analysis. Overall we identified 21 eligiblestudies, of which 15 enrolled one or more comparison groups. The pooled preva-lence of staphylococcal colonization in patients with psoriasis was 353% [95%confidence interval (CI) 250-456] on lesional skin and 392% (95% CI 337-448) in the nares. Patients with psoriasis were 45 times more likely to be colo-nized by S. aureus than healthy controls were on the skin (RR 554, 95% CI 321-957) and 60% more in the nares (RR 160, 95% CI 111-232). Cutaneous andnasal colonization by meticillin-resistant S. aureus also appeared higher in patientswith psoriasis (pooled prevalence 86%) than in healthy controls (26%), yet thedifference was not statistically significant (P = 074). In contrast, despite of a simi-lar risk for nasal staphylococcal colonization (RR 067, 95% CI 038-118), patientswith psoriasis were less likely to carry S. aureus on lesional skin than atopic patients(RR 064, 95% CI 040-102). In summarizing the current literature, we found thatpatients with psoriasis were at an increased risk for staphylococcal colonizationcompared with healthy individuals. Prospective studies on how bacterial loads cor-relate with disease activity can guide the clinical management of bacterial coloniza-tion while preventing the emergence of drug-resistant strains.What's already known about this topic?*Staphylococcus aureus colonizing the skin of patients with psoriasis may trigger or wor-sen the disease activity.

What does this study add?*By summarizing the current literature, we found that patients with psoriasis wereat a significantly increased risk of staphylococcal colonization on lesional skin andin the anterior nares compared with healthy controls.*Patients with psoriasis also seemed to have a similar risk of staphylococcal colo-nization to patients with atopic diseases.Psoriasis vulgaris (psoriasis) is a chronic inflammatory cuta-neous disorder, affecting approximately 2-4% of the generalpopulation.1 While it is well established that Staphylococcus aureusmay exacerbate clinical symptoms of atopic dermatitis viasuperantigen-directed Toll-like receptor-2 pathways and selec-tive stimulation of T-cell expression,2,3 investigations on howstaphylococcal superantigens link to the disease activity of pso-riasis have just begun to accumulate.4-6

Although dysregulation of the skin microbiome and cuta-neous colonization by S. aureus are known to perpetuate psoria-sis via altered immune responses,7,8 bacterial colonization orinfection has received minimal attention in the clinical man-agement of psoriasis.9 This lack of guidance may have resultedfrom two factors: on the one hand, the focus on infections astreatment-associated adverse events rather than as a diseasecomponent in psoriasis,10 and on the other hand, clinicalobservations being inconsistent about the risk of staphylococ-cal colonization in patients with psoriasis.6,11-14To summarize the current literature and to investigate potentialsources of heterogeneous observations, we performed a system-atic review on published risk estimates for S. aureus colonization inpatients with psoriasis. We also quantified risk comparisons byestimating relative risks (RRs) and absolute risk differences (RDs)when comparing patients with psoriasis with healthy controls,and with patients with atopic diseases, separately.Materials and methodsThe conduct of the literature search, critical appraisal, evi-dence synthesis (both qualitatively and quantitatively) andreporting closely followed the published PRISMA reportingguideline.15

Search strategyWe applied database-specific search terms in PubMed, Embase,Web of Science, CINAHL, SCOPUS and the Cochrane Databaseup to December 2014 in the first attempt (Appendix S1; seeSupporting Information). Later, we used the same set ofsearch strategies for an updated search up to February 2016.We did not constrain either search on publication types butlimited the search only to indexed, peer-reviewed journals soas to ensure the quality of publications.We screened titles and abstracts in the English language foreligibility to full-text assessment. For title-only entries, weretrieved the full texts to determine the eligibility unless thetitle clearly indicated its irrelevance, such as studies not inhumans. When a full text was unavailable through member-ship subscription by author-affiliated institutions (Johns Hop-kins Welch Medical Library, Baltimore MD, U.S.A.; ChangGung University, Gueishan, Taiwan; Chang Gung MemorialHospital, Linkou, Taiwan), we excluded it and categorized itas 'inaccessible'. For eligible abstracts without English fulltexts, we browsed through the tables and figures to decidewhether there was sufficient information for inclusion.Study selection and data extractionTwo reviewers (C.F.C. and T.C.W.) independently screened thetitles and abstracts and we resolved inconsistencies based ongroup discussion with a third reviewer (S.H.L. or Y.H.H.), whoalso independently reviewed all titles and abstracts and further

verified the final decision to retrieve full-text articles. Fourreviewers (C.F.C., T.C.W., C.Y.N. and S.H.L.) then indepen-dently screened each full-text article based on three criteria: (i)whether it was an original work as a case series or a cohort ofpatients with psoriasis; (ii) whether the publication reportedrisks of staphylococcal colonization or infection in terms ofprevalence estimates or incidence rates with or without a com-parison group and (iii) the colonization status resulting fromthe conventional culture methods. We excluded review articles,case-control studies in which cases and controls were definedby colonization status, and single-case reports. During theupdated search in February 2016, C.Y.N. and S.H.L. separatelyevaluated the updated search results, and made decisions onobtaining and assessing the eligibility of full-text articles. Therewere no eligible studies added to this updated search.We collected the following information using a structureddata-abstraction form: (i) article information (first author'sname, year of publication and country or city where the studywas conducted; (ii) study population - study setting in whichpatients were enrolled, number of patients included, demo-graphic characteristics of patients, unit of comparison (patient,body site sampled or bacterial isolate); (iii) study design(cross-sectional, pre- vs. postcomparison or longitudinal sur-vey) and (iv) risks of staphylococcal colonization or infectionreported [point estimate and 95% confidence interval (CI) orSE] as ascertained by bacterial culture.Two reviewers (C.F.C. and T.C.W.) independently extracteddata from all articles that were eligible for evidence synthesis.Another two reviewers (C.Y.N. and S.H.L.) separately verifiedand compared the doubly entered data and reached consensusby discussion with a clinical expert on psoriasis (Y.H.H.). Allinvestigators involved in the process of literature search and crit-ical review have been thoroughly trained with skills for databasesearching and critical appraisal of the medical literature.

Reporting quality and risk-of-bias assessmentAs we found that the Newcastle-Ottawa Scale was not readilyapplicable to the included studies,16 we modified the QUA-DAS-2 instrument to assess the reporting quality of individualstudies (Fig. S1; see Supporting Information).17 Two reviewers(C.Y.N. and S.H.L.) independently evaluated each includedpublication, decided the degree of potential risks of bias andscored each item with 1-3 points corresponding to a high-to-low level of risk (Table S1; see Supporting Information).Statistical analysis and meta-analysisWe extracted (or calculated) the published prevalence of staphylo-coccal colonization overall and, when available, by sampling bodysites. We pooled original estimates, including boundary prevalenceestimates (0% or 100%), using fixed-effects (FE) or random-effects(RE) models where appropriate.18 For comparative studies, we cal-culated prevalence differences and prevalence ratios to approximatethe RD and RR of staphylococcal colonization by comparingpatients with psoriasis against healthy controls or atopic patients.

In the meta-analysis, we employed and reported both theinverse variance (FE models) and the DerSimonian and Lairdmethod (RE models) for comparison purposes; we alsoreported the degree of between-study heterogeneity based onthe I2-statistics and results of the Cochrane Q-test to justify thechoice of an FE or RE model.19 For studies with an internalcomparison group (lesional vs. nonlesional) we used RE mod-els to pool cross-site comparisons while accounting forwithin-person correlation. We chose prevalence ratios overodds ratios because the latter tend to overestimate the strengthof associations when the outcome of interest is not a rare con-dition (> 10%) in the study population.20We investigated potential sources of heterogeneity usingseveral approaches. Firstly, we stratified the pooled analysis bypotential confounders at the study level, including the age ofthe study population (adults only vs. children/adolescents ormixed age groups), levels of reporting quality and sources ofstudy populations (inpatient, outpatient, community or 'notreported'). Secondly, we performed univariate metaregressionmodels to identify covariates that explained a significant por-tion of the observed variation among studies. We also usedfunnel plots to examine for publication bias19 and appliedHarbord's modified test for potential small-study effects.21Lastly, we performed influence analysis to identify outliersusing a user-written Stata program ('metainf').22 We per-formed all statistical analyses in Stata 13 (StataCorp, CollegeStation, TX, U.S.A.) at a two-tailed significance level of 005unless otherwise specified.23

ResultsWe identified 581 records in the literature search, andretrieved 202 articles for further full-text assessment (Fig. 1).After excluding 181 articles for various reasons, we included21 eligible studies for qualitative and 19 for quantitative syn-thesis.Study characteristicsNineteen of the 21 publications included were cross-sectionalinvestigations (Table 1); the other two studies performedrepeated sampling but did not achieve complete follow-up.14,24Therefore, we reported only baseline prevalence in Table 1 andin the following meta-analysis. The majority of studies werebased in the U.S.A. (n = 9) or in European countries (n = 8).There were three main sources of patient populations, with themost common source being outpatients (n = 7).6,12,13,25-28Authors of these prevalence studies infrequently reported the(c) 2017 British Association of DermatologistsBritish Journal of Dermatology (2017) 177, pp967-977age or sex distribution of the study population: among 12 stud-ies of known age compositions, nine enrolled only adults aged18 years or older.6,7,13,27,29-32,33Staphylococcal colonization on the skinAmong 21 included studies, 14 reported prevalence estimatesof staphylococcal colonization for skin lesions of patients withpsoriasis (pooled estimate 353%, 95% CI 250-456), includ-ing 11 comparative studies with one or more external com-parison groups and three with internal (cross-body-site)comparisons.6,12,29The current meta-analysis only considered risk comparisonsmade across body sites and with two external control groups:healthy volunteers6,7,13,14,26,28,30,31 and patients with atopicdiseases including atopic dermatitis,14,26,31,32,34-36 eczema11,37and respiratory allergy or asthma.36 We excluded risk compar-ison estimates made relative to patients with other skin orimmune-mediated conditions;11,13,33 we also excluded twostudies in which there were no comparison groups from themeta-analysis (Table S2; see Supporting Information).12,27

Eight studies reported and compared S. aureus colonizationrisks on skin of patients with psoriasis and of their healthycounterparts (Table 2). Among these studies, the prevalenceof staphylococcal colonization was 366% on skin lesions ofpatients with psoriasis (RE estimate, 95% CI 183-548) and51% in healthy controls (FE estimate, 95% CI 30-85). Onaverage, patients with psoriasis consistently showed a 45-foldhigher risk for staphylococcal colonization than healthy indi-viduals (pooled RR 554, 95% CI 321-957; Fig. 2a). Despitesignificant between-study heterogeneities in the absolute scale,patients with psoriasis similarly had a 30% higher risk of car-rying S. aureus than their healthy counterparts (RD 314%,Fig. S2a; see Supporting Information).In contrast to the results in healthy controls, publishedstaphylococcal colonization rates in atopic patients varied sub-stantially across studies (P < 0001 for between-study hetero-geneity, Table 2), with a pooled estimate of 937% (95% CI602-993), whereas the pooled estimate was 450% for thesimilarly enrolled patients with psoriasis (95% CI 313-588).When compared against atopic patients, patients with psoriasiswere 36% less likely to harbour S. aureus on their skin lesions(RR 064, 95% CI 040-102; Fig. 2b), although the differ-ence was statistically significant only in the absolute scale (RD300%, 95% CI 570 to 20; Fig. S2b; see SupportingInformation).In four studies, the investigators compared colonizationrisks between involved vs. uninvolved skin of the same patient(Table 3).6,12,25,29 However, to avoid introducing surveillancebias, we excluded estimates that were based on numbers ofpositive swabs (rather than of patients) from the statisticalpooling.25 The results showed no significant differences in theabsolute colonization burden (95% CI 133-707), althoughlesional sites had a 19-fold higher likelihood of carrying S.aureus than uninvolved skin for a given patient with psoriasis(RR 289, 95% CI 104-804).6,12

Staphylococcal colonization in the naresAccording to four studies reporting the prevalence of nasalstaphylococcal carriage (Table 4),6,13,14,31 495% of patientswith psoriasis (95% CI 400-590) and 267% of healthy par-ticipants (95% CI 107-427) carried S. aureus in the anteriornares. Overall, patients with psoriasis had a 60% higher riskfor nasal staphylococcal colonization than healthy controls (RR160, 95% CI 111-232; Fig. 3a), or by nearly 25% in theabsolute scale (RD 246%, Fig. S3a; see Supporting Informa-tion). Meanwhile, patients with psoriasis appeared to have asimilar risk to atopic patients for nasal staphylococcal coloniza-tion in both the relative (RR 067, 95% CI 038-118;Fig. 3b) and absolute scales (Fig. S3b; see Supporting Infor-mation).Meticillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus colonizationBecause few studies had a sufficient sample size,13,14 wepooled published risk estimates for skin with those for nasalmeticillin-resistantStaphylococcusaureus(MRSA)colonization(Table 5). Overall, the evidence suggested a barely increasedrisk of MRSA colonization in patients with psoriasis vs. healthyindividuals (RD 20%, 95% CI 97-136). Compared withatopic patients, patients with psoriasis were less likely to carryMRSA on the skin lesions (RD 62%) or in the nares (RD54%), yet the differences were of only borderline signifi-cance (Table 5).

Quality assessment and impactsIn stratified analysis, the results of risk comparison betweenpatients with psoriasis and healthy controls (or atopic patients)did not differ substantially from the overall findings, despitelarge uncertainties in certain subgroups by age (Fig. S4; seeSupporting Information) and by source populations (Fig. S5).Results from low-quality reports did not deviate from those ofhigh-quality ones but were less reliable (Fig. S6). No study-level characteristics seemed to correlate with between-studyvariations in meta-regression models (data not shown). Whilethere was no statistical evidence of small-study effects as indi-cated by the modified Harbord's test, several studies lay out-side of the 95% CI in funnel plots (Fig. S7; see SupportingInformation); however, exclusion of each did not considerablyalter the meta-analysis results (Fig. S8).DiscussionWe systematically identified and synthesized the current litera-ture on staphylococcal colonization in patients with psoriasis.We showed that more than one-third of patients with psoria-sis carried S. aureus on their skin lesions (pooled estimate353%). When compared with healthy individuals, patientswith psoriasis had a significantly increased risk of staphylococ-cal colonization either on the skin (RR 554) or in the nares(RR 160). Casas 20117GermanyUnclear39 psoriasis and 39 healthy volunteers54 (26-75)NALesional 46%8(continued)(c) 2017 British Association of DermatologistsBritish Journal of Dermatology (2017) 177, pp967-977than that in atopic disease, particularly in the communitycohorts.14,36 According to a limited number of studies,patients with psoriasis had a comparable risk for MRSA colo-nization with healthy controls; they were less likely than ato-pic patients to carry these drug-resistant strains (RR 017,95% CI 003-095).Over the past decade, a growing body of evidence hasshown that pathogen-host interactions involving both theinnate and the adaptive immune pathways are likely to beamong the driving forces underlying the dysfunctional Thelper (Th)1/Th17 immune axis in psoriasis.38-41 An earlyfinding of V-B element-bearing T cells enriched in psoriaticlesions led to the speculative role of microbial superantigensin the pathogenesis of psoriasis.42,43 Such speculation waslater supported by observation of an increased prevalence ofstaphylococcal superantigen in the lesional skin of patientswith psoriasis compared with skin of healthy controls.6

Using culture-independent techniques, researchers have alsoshown that the relative abundance of S. aureus, along withother Firmicutes44 or other types of Staphylococcus,45 wasincreased in lesional skin compared with nonlesional sites ofpatients with psoriasis (or with healthy skin), although therewere reports to the contrary.46 As the consensus on how toStudyCountry and studyperiodPatient sourceStudy populationAge (years),mean (range)Male(%)Staphylococcusaureuscolonization (%)QualityscoreGraber 201114FrancecCommunity31 children with chronic dermatoses, including7 AD and 5 psoriasis; 19 family members ascontrolsMarcus 201124U.S.A., 16 monthsInpatient98 samples from 73 patients with psoriasisdNANALesional 32%3Atefi 201228Iran, 2006-2009Outpatient40 patients and 40 controls43 (9-86)e65%Lesional 75%5Andersen 201336Denmark, 2008-2011Community2196 Danish twins, including 112 withpsoriasis and 607 with atopic diseases (AD,asthma, allergy)NANANasal 375%6Varley 201433U.S.A.Inpatient548 IMID, including 285 with psoriasis and154 with RA48b (18-79)49%Combined 435%f3AD, atopic dermatitis; CTCL, cutaneous T-cell lymphoma; HIE, hyper-IgE syndrome; IMID, immune-mediated inflammatory diseases; MRSA, meticillin-resistant Staphylococcus aureus; NA, not available; RA,rheumatoid arthritis. aResults were based on 92 lesional samples taken from 61 patients and 52 nonlesional samples taken from 50 (out of 61) patients. bMedian age. cThree consecutive annual summercamps in June 2005, 2006 and 2007; only findings in 2005 were shown. dForty-two of whom were sampled twice or more. eOf patients with psoriasis; controls were age and sex matched. fNares andinguinal folds were sampled.PsoriasisHealthy controls

I2 or s2 (P forheterogeneity)892% (P < 0001)041 (P = 013)I2 or s2 (P forheterogeneity)618% (P = 0016)797 (P < 0001)aSamples were taken from skin with active lesions or from handsof participants with psoriasis.(c) 2017 British Association of DermatologistsBritish Journal of Dermatology (2017) 177, pp967-977conduct microbiome research on dermatological disorders hasjust begun to build,47 we expect to see a more and morecomprehensive characterization of the skin microbial commu-nity that is associated with particular diseases at the onset, inremission or in relapse.In comparison with a recently published systematic reviewon staphylococcal colonization in patients with atopic dermati-tis,48 original epidemiological investigations of the psoriasispopulation have been relatively few and poorly conducted orreported (Table S1; see Supporting Information). Despite theStudyAge (years)Male (%)Colonized/total (%)

RD (95% CI)RR (95% CI)LesionalNonlesionalCI, confidence interval; NA, not available; RD, risk difference; RR, relative risk.(c) 2017 British Association of DermatologistsBritish Journal of Dermatology (2017) 177, pp967-977fact that more than half of the studies recruited at least oneexternal comparison group, most investigators did not providesufficient details on the participant selection and enrolmentprocedures, and whether culture results were interpreted inde-pendently of clinical diagnoses (Fig. S1; see Supporting Infor-mation). Both, if unduly executed, would result in biasedassessments of absolute and relative risks.17As S. aureus can reside among the human host's skin andnasal microbiota in the general population, with a prevalenceofasymptomaticnasalcolonizationapproximating20-30%,49,50 an adequately selected (or matched) control grouphas a pivotal role in deriving valid risk comparisons. Well-known confounders, such as male sex, younger age, race/eth-nicity and chronic medical conditions including diabetes mel-litus and obesity,51 should therefore be adjusted for toprovide valid and reliable risk estimates. Yet none of the stud-ies in the current review addressed these potential confound-ing effects.Among the original studies included in the current review,the study populations were poorly characterized and thepatientspectrumvariedfromstudytostudy,possiblyStudyPrevalence (%)PsoriasisHealthy

StudyPsoriasisAtopic disease(a)(b)(c) 2017 British Association of DermatologistsBritish Journal of Dermatology (2017) 177, pp967-977contributing to the large between-study variation observed inthe prevalence estimates and risk comparisons. For example,specific subtypes of psoriasis (or of atopic diseases), diseaseduration and current or previous therapeutic agents wereimportant clinical variables that might be associated with indi-vidual risks for bacterial colonization. Despite the fact thatmost investigators reported that they collected skin swabsbefore treatment at the outpatient clinic or on the first day ofadmission into the hospital, inadequate reporting has pre-cluded possible subgroup analysis to identify further anypotential prognostic or protective factors for staphylococcalcolonization in patients with psoriasis.Moreover, the number of sampling swabs collected perpatient often outnumbered that from a healthy control. Suchoversampling bias could be avoided by defining a fixed num-ber of sampling body sites a priori for both patients andhealthy controls. When different numbers of swabs per personwere collected (as was typical in retrospective clinical stud-ies),24,25 reporting of culture findings at the person level,rather than at the swab level, might reduce the risk forsurveillance bias (although not completely).We also caution that the study findings should be inter-preted in light of a number of methodological limitationsassociated with the review process. All risk comparisons con-sidered were cross-sectional by design; when the authorsreported both pre- and post-treatment prevalence rates, weincluded only the former in the current review and meta-ana-lysis. How bacterial colonization in patients with psoriasisevolved over time, with or without clinical treatment, wasbeyond the scope of the current review. In addition, the colo-nization prevalence of S. aureus varied over the sampling bodysites,butfewstudiesreportedsite-specificresults.14,37

Although we recognized that S. aureus might show preferencefor specific skin types (dry, wet or oily),52 our results per-tained only to an overall assessment of staphylococcal colo-nization in patients with psoriasis.The majority of the study populations considered were hos-pital-based outpatients. Future population-based, communitysurveys are required to provide further reliable evidence onwhether patients with psoriasis serve as a hidden reservoir forthe emergence of community-associated MRSA.31,50 Lastly,much as this review was limited to the published literatureand there were potential publication biases, we did not findevidence that was suggestive of this bias based on funnelplots, Harbord's modified test or influence analysis.In conclusion, based on the current literature, we havedemonstrated that patients with psoriasis have an increasedrisk of S. aureus colonization on the skin and in the anteriornares compared with healthy individuals. Such heightened riskfor cutaneous bacterial burden is no less than that observed inatopic patients. While more mechanistic research is needed toinvestigate the aetiological role of S. aureus in the natural his-tory of psoriasis, prospective investigations that seek to informclinicians of optimal practices to constrain bacterial coloniza-tion and to prevent the emergence of drug-resistant strains arealso warranted.References1 Parisi R, Symmons DP, Griffiths CE et al.
